topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the glossy paper with a fingertip, the edges catching the harsh fluorescent light of the library study carrels. It was supposed to be a draft, a collection of tentative thoughts she’d scribbled late one night. Instead, the typeface was pristine, the narrative polished to an almost painful perfection. The essay detailed a quiet moment of realization about the resilience of overlooked things, a theme she had only vaguely touched upon in her own writing. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, making her breath catch in her throat. She reread the opening paragraph, the words flowing effortlessly, and felt a strange, hollow ache bloom behind her ribs. Someone else had captured the exact shade of vulnerability she was trying so desperately to hide. She quickly slid the document back into her bag, the leather cool against her palm, and walked out into the noisy campus square, the sound of distant laughter feeling amplified and sharp.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, his knuckles white where he gripped the mouse. The document glowed on his monitor, an essay titled "The Geometry of Silence," complete with sophisticated metaphors and a profound sense of introspection. It was his. He’d poured weeks into wrestling with the concept of finding peace in chaos, and here it was, someone else’s eloquent articulation of that very struggle. A tremor ran through his hands, making the keyboard feel slick and foreign beneath his touch. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap vinyl squeaking under his weight, and closed his eyes, trying to filter the intrusion. When he opened them, the words seemed to mock him—so articulate, so unguarded. He felt a sudden, intense urge to crumple the paper, to erase the evidence of his own messy, unedited interior life.

[story 3]

When Chloe found the file tucked away in a shared drive, the initial shock was a dull, heavy thud in her chest. It was her application essay, meticulously crafted, published under a pseudonym by an anonymous student. The prose was breathtakingly honest, a perfect distillation of a feeling she had only ever managed to sketch out in fragmented notes. Her jaw tightened, and she unconsciously pressed the heels of her hands against her eyes, trying to physically block the image from fully registering. The air in the cramped dorm room suddenly felt thick and hard to draw in. She stood up abruptly, needing distance, needing to move away from the proximity of that flawless expression of her own internal landscape.
